name: The Quirky Dataset Hunt
tagline: let the model find you something fun to work with
move: Tell the model to search online AND use what it knows of your interests.
Constrain: small (~10k-100k rows), varied columns (numbers, categories, longish text).
Require that the resulting STORY interest a wide audience; quirky welcome.
Ask for several candidates, then pick one as a group.
why: A good dataset is most of a good story; keeping it small and varied makes your first attempt easy to finish.
try_it: Everyone generates 5 candidates; the group picks one.
watch_for: Models default to tutorial datasets (Titanic, Iris) - insist on something unseen.

examples.context: Under the heading 'Prompt to find a dataset' is the verbatim prompt he uses to open a data-viz workshop: it asks the model to search online and use his past interests to suggest small (~10k-100k row), column-varied, quirky datasets whose story would grip a mixed audience. This is the technique in its purest form - the model, not the human, finds the raw material.
Published slides for the 'Prompt to Plot' workshop where this dataset-finding prompt is run live before any analysis, demonstrating that the very first step (sourcing data) is delegated to the LLM.

name: Data Hunt Brief
tagline: make AI find the evidence before it tells the story
move: Ask: 'Find public datasets for [question].'
Require a table: source owner, URL, update frequency, fields, geography, granularity, format, licence, caveats, 3 angles.
Prefer primary sources; instruct the model to flag anything uncertain.
why: Check where the data comes from - its licence, freshness and limits - before you build a story on it.
try_it: Ask 'Where can I get district-level data on women's labour-force participation in India?'
watch_for: Dashboards often display data without exposing a clean downloadable file.

examples.context: The 'Prompt to find a dataset' section shows the habit of having AI surface and qualify candidate datasets up front; the Data Hunt Brief is the disciplined, provenance-table version of that same move.

GIJN's GIJC25 session 'Uncovering Wrongdoing Using AI' documents investigative teams defining a data source and topics and having AI return structured, vetted leads with provenance - the professional standard this technique mirrors.

name: The Source Sweep
tagline: dig through official data for stories nobody has reported yet
move: List 6-8 authoritative/primary sources for the beat.
Ask for 5 SURPRISING, not-widely-reported comparative insights.
Filter: only a clear #1 outlier, a dramatic before/after, or an unexpected vs-the-world position qualifies.
Return each as structured JSON: headline, 3-sentence story, the surprise, data points, source links.
why: The explicit filter criteria force the model past famous facts into front-page territory.
try_it: Run it on one beat; reject every insight you already knew.
watch_for: Figures may be invented - nothing is true until traced to the cited source.

examples.context: In the section 'Statnostics, 2 Mar 2026 -> Find stories via Codex' is the full research-assistant prompt: it lists NCRB, MOSPI, RBI, NFHS, World Gold Council etc., demands 5 surprising unreported insights filtered to outlier/before-after/vs-world, and outputs each as an insight.json. The folders it generated (crash-severity-paradox, rural-consumption-catchup, remittance-rank-paradox) are listed right below it, showing the technique produced real story leads.

name: The Editor's Pick
tagline: sift a news feed for the stories that will still matter next week
move: Paste a news list or feed.
Ask the model to think like a veteran editor and pick items that still interest readers in two weeks, not just today.
Require a ranked top-10 with reasons and an analytical angle for each.
why: Separates durable analysis from disposable breaking news.
try_it: Feed today's headlines from one outlet; pick the one with the longest shelf life.
watch_for: The model favours drama - check the suggested angle is actually researchable.

examples.context: Under 'West Bengal's Voter Roll Revision -> Research', he pastes the Times News Network newslist and asks the model to think like a Times of India editor and select the 10 stories that would still interest readers a week or two later, ranked with reasons and angles. The same prompt was run across GPT, Claude and Gemini (chat links are in the file).

name: Scrape It With Code
tagline: turn any page into a dataset
move: Ask for console JavaScript you can paste in DevTools to copy a page's content to the clipboard.
Paste the captured content back; ask the model to write code that scrapes it into an array of JSON objects.
why: Unlocks sources with no download button - the model writes the scraper so you don't have to code.
try_it: Scrape a public leaderboard or a WhatsApp group export.
watch_for: Respect terms of service and privacy; never scrape behind logins or paywalls.

examples.context: The 'Scraping WhatsApp' section contains the exact prompt: ask ChatGPT for console JS that copies the WhatsApp-web HTML to the clipboard, paste it back, then have it write code to scrape each message into JSON. The model produces the scraper end to end.
Published slides ('Data Design by Dialogue') that walk through the full scrape -> clean -> model -> visualize chain with this scraping step as the opener.

His published 'Scraping SEC' walkthrough is this technique taken to its conclusion: a single Codex CLI session writes an SEC-filings scraper, recovers from messy data and its own errors, and documents every step - the model writing and debugging the whole scraper so the human never has to.

name: Trend-to-Assignment Monitor
tagline: watch live data and get pinged when something becomes a story
move: Define the topics/sources to watch (search trends, a public dashboard, a registry diff).
Have an agent re-query on a schedule and flag only newsworthy changes.
For each flag, return: why it matters, data to fetch, one chart idea, one headline, one reason it's a non-story.
why: Converts a static data source into a continuous lead generator.
try_it: Convert one trending public issue into a data assignment.
watch_for: Spikes are often memes, outrage cycles or bot activity, not durable stories.

examples.context: GIJN's GIJC25 write-up describes a Norway fisheries reporter who predefines topics (cod catches in northern Norway, new vessel registrations, regulatory changes) so an AI system generates customized updates from a database - a standing monitor that turns a data feed into recurring story leads. That is exactly this technique in production.

name: Match-Your-Data
tagline: cross-check your own list against a public database to find matches
move: Upload your own list (names, companies, addresses).
Upload or point to a reference dataset (a registry, a leaks database).
Ask the model to find overlaps and likely matches, each with a confidence flag.
why: The 'who in my data appears in that data' join is how many investigations break open.
try_it: Match a local list of officials against a public asset or company registry.
watch_for: Fuzzy matches produce false positives - every hit needs human confirmation before naming anyone.

examples.context: ICIJ built a tool letting reporters match their own data - including names and addresses - against more than 810,000 offshore entities from its leaks. It is the canonical implementation of joining your list to a public reference set to surface leads.

ICIJ used machine learning to find passports across a massive document leak and build per-country lists of clients and beneficial owners - the same entity-matching move at scale, and a model for how the technique pays off in real reporting.

name: Hypothesis Forge
tagline: ask sharp, testable questions instead of poking around at random
move: Give the model column names, sample rows and summary stats; ask it to act as a data editor for a named audience.
Have it generate ~12 sharply testable hypotheses.
For each: the exact test, columns needed, expected direction, confounders, chart idea, headline-if-true, headline-if-false.
Rank by surprising + defensible.
why: Moves analysis from 'describe everything' to 'test what might matter' - the logic of his own one-click app.
try_it: Upload a small airline, election or cricket dataset and generate hypotheses.
watch_for: Some hypotheses sound clever but can't be tested with the data at hand.

examples.context: Hypoforge is the app he built that productizes this technique: you upload a dataset and it automatically proposes and tests hypotheses, doing hypothesis-first analysis instead of open-ended exploration.

In this talk transcript he demos 'Hypothesis Forge' live - uploading DGCA daily airline-traffic data and letting the app drive a hypothesis-driven analysis, explicitly because neither the business nor the analyst knows what to ask up front.

name: The Expert's Eye
tagline: the highest-leverage one-liner in the catalogue
move: Append to almost any analysis prompt: 'What would an expert in this field check or recognise that a beginner would miss?'
Also ask: 'What questions would an expert ask? What failures would they anticipate?'
why: Pulls the model out of generic mode into domain expertise for almost no effort.
try_it: Add it to a finding you've already analyzed and see what it catches.
watch_for: It can invent plausible-sounding 'expert' caveats - keep only the real ones.

examples.context: The 'West Bengal -> Research' prompt contains this exact expert-lens block appended to a story-selection task: 'What patterns would an expert in this field check / recognize that beginners would miss? What questions would an expert ask that a beginner would not? What problems / failures would an expert anticipate?' - showing the one-liner bolted onto a real editorial prompt.

name: BIG-USEFUL-SURPRISING Ranker
tagline: choose the story, not the chart
move: Score candidate insights 1-5 on Big, Useful, Surprising, Defensible (optionally Visual, Emotional, Actionable).
Rank them.
Make the model justify why the top 3 should be pursued and the bottom 3 dropped.
why: Makes editorial judgment explicit and teachable rather than tacit.
try_it: Generate 12 findings from one dataset, then rank them on the rubric.
watch_for: AI overvalues novelty; the human still judges reader usefulness.

examples.context: His data-analysis skill's 'Prioritize & Package' section defines exactly this rubric: select insights that are high-impact, actionable, surprising and defensible, and lead with the most compelling. The technique operationalizes his own packaging criteria as a scoring prompt.

The insight.json filter criteria used in 'Find stories via Codex' (only a clear #1 outlier, a dramatic before/after, or an unexpected vs-world position qualifies) are a lightweight version of the same ranked editorial judgment.

name: Schema Forge
tagline: have AI document what every column means before you clean it
move: Have the model infer each column's meaning, type, unit, allowed range, missingness, primary/foreign keys, joins and PII/sensitive fields.
Have it list likely data-quality checks.
Output a data dictionary plus validation rules.
why: Turns tacit analyst memory into a reviewable artifact others can check.
try_it: Upload a messy multi-sheet government Excel file.
watch_for: Inferred joins and meanings can be wrong - verify with counts and sample rows.

examples.context: This is an AI-generated data dictionary for the Lancet Citizens' Commission survey: it documents all 232 columns organised by section (A=demographics, B=outpatient, C=inpatient, D=preferences), two critical code-crosswalk tables, key stats, script usage, and a 'Critical Data Pitfalls' section - exactly the reviewable schema artifact this technique produces.

name: Dirty Data Treasure Hunt
tagline: errors in the data are either bugs to fix or stories to tell
move: Run a data-quality investigation for impossible values, duplicate identities, unit mixups, date breaks, monotonicity breaks, denominator shifts, name/geography mismatches, outlier clusters and aggregation leaks.
For each issue, show evidence, example rows, severity, likely cause, a fix/exclude rule, and whether it might itself be a story.
why: Errors reveal both data risk and institutional behaviour - the bug can be the lede.
try_it: Ask it to find 'one impossible row and one systemic issue.'
watch_for: Check the official data definition before publishing an anomaly as a finding.

examples.context: The 'Cleaning Data' section shows the prompt 'Here are WhatsApp messages. Any missing values?' and the model's reply (reactions blank ~64%, author null ~6% from system messages, time null ~5.5% from parsing glitches), then it fixes time by interpolation and returns a cleaned file - the quality investigation in miniature.

The 'Critical Data Pitfalls' section documents five data errors that caused verification failures - e.g. the c1_most provider codes are swapped, so if Government median out-of-pocket expense shows Rs 15,000 the codes are reversed. A data anomaly that, uncaught, would have produced a false story: the treasure-hunt as both fix and warning.

name: Structure the Unstructured
tagline: messy text/PDF/images into a clean table
move: Feed PDFs, scanned forms or freeform text.
Ask the model to extract specified fields into a consistent table.
Spot-check a sample of the extraction by hand.
why: Unlocks the ~90% of the world's data that isn't already in spreadsheets.
try_it: Turn 20 messy press releases into one tidy table of who-announced-what-when.
watch_for: Extraction quality drops on poor scans and odd layouts - hand-check ~10%.

examples.context: ICIJ turned millions of unstructured leaked pages into structured, searchable lists of passport and beneficial-owner records, giving partner journalists clean per-country starting points. It is the flagship example of converting document chaos into a usable table.

In 'Modeling Topics', Claude Code writes topics.py to embed each free-text WhatsApp message, cluster the embeddings, and name the clusters, producing a tagged.json that adds structure (a cluster label) to every message - structuring messy text into an analyzable table.

name: Always Write Code, Never Eyeball
tagline: the foundational habit
move: Never ask the model to 'read' the numbers.
Always instruct: 'write and run code to compute this.'
Then read the code, not just the answer.
why: Models hallucinate arithmetic but write reliable scripts; this one habit separates reproducible analysis from confident error.
try_it: Ask the same question both ways - 'what's the average?' vs 'write code to compute the average' - and compare.
watch_for: A wrong filter produces a clean-looking wrong number - inspect the code.

examples.context: The recurring 'Tactics I learnt' refrain states it bluntly: 'LLMs err. Have them write code!' and 'Have LLMs Write code to analyze.' The 'Modeling Topics' example shows the principle in action - he has Claude Code write topics.py to do the computation (cost $0.67, 22 minutes) rather than asking the model to eyeball results.

name: Ask for a Dozen, Not One
tagline: breadth before depth
move: Ask for 10-12 diverse data stories from one dataset, explicitly inviting quirky ones.
Have the model write code to analyze each and interpret it as a story with surprise and human appeal.
Triage to the best one or two.
why: The best story is rarely the first one; volume lets you triage to the gem.
try_it: Generate twelve, keep two.
watch_for: Quantity invites shallowness - make it prove each with code, not vibes.

examples.context: The 'Visualizing Stories' prompt reads: 'List 10 diverse data stories we can explore. Include quirky ones. Then, for each, write the code to analyze the data... interpret each as a story with surprise & human appeal. Amuse me!' and the tactic note adds 'Don't ask for one. Ask for a dozen analyses.' - the technique stated and demonstrated together.

name: Test Them All
tagline: turn every hypothesis into code and results
move: Convert each hypothesis into an executable test: write code, run it, return result, confidence, caveat and a simple chart.
If code fails, debug and retry.
End with a table: hypothesis, supported/rejected/mixed, evidence, chart, story potential.
why: Converts editorial curiosity into a repeatable analysis loop (pairs with Hypothesis Forge).
try_it: Generate 10 hypotheses, then ask the model to test all 10.
watch_for: Code can run correctly while testing the wrong thing - inspect the test definition.

examples.context: The 'Prompt to analyze & Visualize' instruction tells the model to run all the analyses with statistical hypothesis testing and 'if something is not statistically significant, then flag those off, drop those analyses' - i.e. execute every candidate as a real test and prune the ones that don't hold.

name: The Surprise Hunt
tagline: look systematically where stories hide
move: Walk the model through the checklist: tails and outliers, pattern breaks, surprising correlations, standout entities, hidden subgroups (Simpson's paradox), unlikely field combinations.
Flag the one finding that makes a reader say 'wait, really?'
why: Structured hunting beats 'find something interesting.'
try_it: Run the full checklist; flag the single most surprising result.
watch_for: A subgroup finding on a tiny n is noise dressed as signal - always check the count.

examples.context: The 'Hunt for Signal' section enumerates exactly where to look: extreme/unexpected distributions and tails, pattern breaks, surprising correlations, standout over/under-performers, hidden populations (with a Simpson's-paradox warning) and dot-connections across unrelated fields - the literal checklist this technique runs.

The citizen-survey insight list is the output of such a hunt: e.g. 99.8% of food-spend answers are multiples of Rs 100 (a rounding fingerprint), and 39.5% of women vs 1.3% of men are coded into 'domestic chores' (women's-work invisibility) - surprising signals pulled from the tails and subgroups.

'IMDb's Hidden Algorithm Bias' is a published surprise pulled straight from a hidden subgroup: popular new films score lower not because of any algorithm but because casual viewers dilute the enthusiasm of devoted fans - a Simpson's-paradox-style twist that only appears once you split the audience, exactly the kind of finding this checklist hunts for.

name: Interview the Corpus (with receipts)
tagline: question a pile of documents, with a citation for every answer
move: Load the documents; summarize the corpus first.
Plan searches across people, money, dates, contradictions and missing records; run each thread separately.
Require a document/page citation for every answer.
End with confirmed facts, open questions, contradictions and follow-up reporting tasks.
why: The best AI investigations keep four steps apart: understand the documents, plan the searches, pull the evidence, then write it up.
try_it: Load 10 annual reports or court filings and interrogate them.
watch_for: Never trust uncited synthesis - it confabulates page numbers and quotes.

examples.context: The 'Research West Bengal SIR' prompt is a research-analyst brief that interrogates a source set across precedent, legal status, geographic distribution and ground-level testimony, prioritises primary sources (ECI orders, Supreme Court filings, gazette notifications), and demands a structured memo of key findings, data gaps and questions needing on-ground reporting - querying a corpus like a witness, with sourcing.

name: Entity & Network Mapping
tagline: extract who-connects-to-whom
move: Ask the model to pull people, organisations and relationships from text into nodes and edges.
Visualize the result as a graph.
Look for brokers, clusters and conflicts of interest.
why: Networks reveal connectors and hidden ties that tables conceal.
try_it: Map the co-directors in a dataset and find the node everyone routes through.
watch_for: Name disambiguation is hard - one name may be three people or three names one person.

examples.context: ICIJ's data-journalism work (its Datashare platform with Neo4j graph-building) links people and corporate entities to quickly surface hidden ownership networks across leaks - the reference implementation of turning documents into an entity graph.
The 'Modeling Topics' step builds an embedding-similarity structure over messages (shown in similarity.webm) - the same node/edge thinking applied to text to reveal which items cluster together.

name: Red-Team Your Own Story
tagline: attack your own finding before anyone else can
move: Tell the model to act as a hostile data editor.

Have it find every reason the conclusion could be wrong: wrong denominator, missing base rate, survivorship/selection bias, confounders, cherry-picking, Simpson's paradox, stale data, alternative explanations.
Make it mark each kill/weaken/check/ignore and cite the exact row, chart or claim.
why: Critique prompts trigger different behaviour than storytelling prompts; a finding that survives is publishable.
try_it: Run it on the room's strongest insight.
watch_for: The model is agreeable by default - explicitly instruct it to argue against you and cite specifics.

examples.context: The citizen-survey verification thread is real red-teaming: after drafting six cards he had AI download the raw Harvard Dataverse data and re-verify, which caught that a headline claim of '1 in 4' was actually '1 in 7' and that swapped provider codes would have produced a false story. Attacking the finding against source data before publishing is this technique in practice.
The 'Verify & Stress-Test' section codifies the attack, including Inversion: 'try to disprove the finding. If you can't, it's more credible' - the mindset the hostile-editor prompt invokes.

name: Placebo Shuffle
tagline: check your finding isn't just a fluke
move: Stress-test the finding with: a shuffled-target placebo, minimum-sample sensitivity, segment-wise analysis, bootstrap intervals, outlier removal, alternative denominators, Simpson's-paradox checks.
Return claim, test, pass/fail, changed result, caveat.
why: A finding must survive the easy ways of being wrong.
try_it: Test whether a 'hidden champion' metric survives after removing tiny samples.
watch_for: Robustness reduces fragility; it does not prove causality.

examples.context: The 'Test robustness' bullet states the move precisely: check whether the finding holds under alternative specs, thresholds and sub-samples, and run 'a placebo test (shuffled labels, random baseline). If [it reproduces], it's noise.' That is the Placebo Shuffle written as a standing instruction.

name: Evidence Packet
tagline: every claim gets a receipt
move: For every claim in the draft, attach: source table/file, row/filter, calculation, chart ID, caveat and confidence.
Flag any sentence that is unsupported, overstated, or missing a denominator.
why: Keeps polished writing from hiding weak evidence - the thing that makes AI-assisted work trustworthy.
try_it: Annotate a 300-word data brief with a receipt per claim.
watch_for: A citation to a report is not enough for a number - cite the table and the calculation.

examples.context: The MOSPI 'Add citations' instruction is an Evidence Packet generator: create a .md alongside each SVG that is 'a comprehensive verification checklist & SOP', giving the journalist text to copy-paste-search, the actual numbers to verify against, and how to re-derive each figure from the source MOSPI PLFS data.

The published Statnostics gallery puts a 'Verify' button on every card that opens its SOP/citation file, so each headline number ships with a receipt a reader can independently check - the technique made part of the published product.

name: One Stat, One Square
tagline: the social card format
move: Brief a square (1:1) minimalist poster: one headline insight, one small secondary footnote.
Bold typography, simple icons, ample negative space; instantly readable, not a detailed infographic.
Keep enforcing 'sparser' until it's a single takeaway.
why: A fixed format can be reused again and again - the same card works on social media and in print.
try_it: Turn one insight card into a square poster.
watch_for: Models over-pack by default - push back repeatedly.

examples.context: The format spec defines the card precisely: a square artboard built around one memorable statistical takeaway - branded masthead, a strong 1-2 line headline, a short deck, one main visual, a tiny source line. It is the blueprint for the one-stat square.

The Gemini 'Version 2a' feedback loop shows the distillation in action: after rejecting busy infographics ('too elaborate'), he asks for a square, minimalist poster with ONE main headline insight and one smaller footnote - and notes 'This works well.'

name: The See-and-Fix Loop
tagline: make the model inspect its own output
move: Have the model generate one chart, convert it to an image, and view it.
Have it fix any text overlap or misalignment it sees.
Pause for human feedback before generating the rest.
why: Broken layouts and overlapping text appear only in the rendered image, never in the code.
try_it: Generate -> render -> critique -> regenerate a single card end to end.
watch_for: Never trust raw SVG/code - always inspect the picture.

examples.context: The MOSPI Statoistics thread repeats this loop verbatim: 'To start with, generate one SVG, view the output, fix any errors you find, and pause to check with me for feedback' and 'test it visually by converting it into an image and viewing it' - the model is made to look at its own rendered output before continuing.

name: Chart Menu with Reasons
tagline: let AI propose several charts and say which one fits best
move: Ask for ~8 visual treatments for the claim: chart type, encoding, interaction, reader question answered, why it fits, why it may fail, data shape required.
Have the model pick the best and explain why it beats the alternatives.
(For your own exploration instead, ask for a dozen charts or a scatterplot matrix and mine it.)
why: Turns chart choice into an editorial decision, not decoration.
try_it: One claim, three audiences, three different charts.
watch_for: Models love maps - geography has to actually matter.

examples.context: The citizen-survey brief encodes the fit-first principle: 'Don't use traditional bar charts and line charts for everything - think about the best way to visualize each insight. But make sure the visualizations are especially appropriate, not just fancy.' - choosing the chart by the insight, not habit.

name: Win-an-Award Restyle
tagline: invoke the NYT graphics desk
move: Tell the model: 'Imagine you're the New York Times graphics team.'
Ask what fonts, palette and design choices it would make; note default matplotlib is ugly.
Ask it to make the result beautiful and award-winning.
why: Naming a gold standard lifts output several notches for free.
try_it: Restyle a plain chart with this single instruction.
watch_for: Pretty isn't a story - pair it with the narrative spine.

examples.context: The 'Prompt to improve visual' reads: 'Imagine you are writing a New York Times data visualization. How would you go about it? What fonts... color schemes... design choices?... write it in a way that will get me an award.' - the restyle invocation in full.
The 'Let's jam!' prompts include 'Redraw with Seaborn (not matplotlib) and make it beautiful! Award winning!!' - the same gold-standard escalation applied mid-session.

name: Novel Viz Generator
tagline: out of bar-line-scatter autopilot
move: Ask the model to invent ~10 non-standard visual metaphors (no bar/line/scatter/map).
For each: metaphor, encoding, interaction, reader task, likely confusion, implementation complexity.
Build the simplest credible one.
(Variant: ask for a chart 'invented today' to push the model off its priors.)
why: AI is excellent for design-space exploration before feasibility filtering.
try_it: Ask for 'bad but interesting' visuals, then salvage one.
watch_for: Novelty that reduces comprehension is decoration, not storytelling.

examples.context: His published 'novel data visualizations' experiment - deliberately non-standard chart forms generated and refined with LLMs, demonstrating the design-space exploration this technique is for.

The talk opens by pushing the model with low-prior 'stretch questions' - 'Can it create a chart newly invented today?' and asking it to build a SOM or invent a visualization that does not yet exist - precisely to escape bar/line/scatter defaults.

name: Sketchnotes & Funny Infographics
tagline: image-model posters for social
move: Ask an image model to summarize the insight as a rich, colorful, funny sketchnote - pick the key points, structure them, then draw.
Try variants: a funny infographic poster, or a single-panel comic.
Keep any data claim minimal and verify it on the card.
why: Image models make shareable, human, memorable graphics that chat tools can't.
try_it: Generate a sketchnote and a 'serious' infographic of the same insight; compare reactions.
watch_for: Image models can't reliably render precise numbers or labels - keep claims minimal and check them.

examples.context: The 'Visualize using Gemini Pro' section holds the exact image prompts and his verdicts: the sketchnote prompt ('visually rich, intricately detailed, colorful, and funny, sketchnote... think about the most important points... then draw it') which 'worked well', an RK-Laxman single-panel comic prompt (worked only sometimes), and a funny-infographic prompt ('worked OK'). It documents both the prompts and which image formats actually land.

name: Prompt-to-Plot Sprint
tagline: build the first visual story before overthinking
move: Ask for a one-page interactive story from a CSV using browser-native HTML/CSS/JS.
Sections: hook, method, main chart, outlier drilldown, caveats.
Keep the data local; have it comment each calculation and design choice.
why: A working prototype reveals story structure faster than a slide deck.
try_it: 30 minutes: each group builds one visual story from the same data.
watch_for: Pretty prototypes can hide wrong transforms - inspect the calculations.

examples.context: The 'Prompt to Plot' workshop where a mixed, largely non-technical audience each built a one-page interactive data story from a shared CSV entirely via LLMs - the sprint in its native habitat.

The 'Visualizations Created' section lists the live participant outputs (e.g. several booksviz pages) built end-to-end during the session, evidence that a fast prototype-first approach produces shippable stories.

name: Format-as-Spec (Newsroom Automation)
tagline: write the rules once, generate forever
move: Have the model study a few exemplar outputs and document a reusable format spec (layout, palette, fonts, masthead).
Then batch-generate new cards against that spec.
Lock brand-critical rules as MANDATORY so the model doesn't drift.
why: The 'automate a newsroom function' move - consistent output at scale.
try_it: Codify your house style, then produce five cards from five findings.
watch_for: Without MANDATORY brand rules (masthead, colours), the model drifts off-style.

examples.context: The 'Analyze SVGs' prompt is the spec-writing step: 'Analyze the SVGs. Document the content and format concisely in statoistics-format.md clearly enough for someone else to create new SVGs in the same style - but leaving enough room for improvements.' He has the model write the reusable rulebook.

The resulting spec - square layout conventions, print-friendly palette, larger-font rules, and a MANDATORY masthead specification (exact 'STATOISTICS' wordmark colours and title-bar dimensions) - is what every later batch of cards is generated against, turning style into automation.

name: Make-the-Reader-Do-the-Work
tagline: guess-then-reveal, explorable, personalized
move: Build interaction that earns its place: let readers draw their guess before the reveal, manipulate a variable, or see 'in your city / at your income.'
Make the default (no-interaction) view already communicate the core insight.
why: Active prediction beats passive reading for retention; the gap between guess and truth is the story.
try_it: Add a 'draw your guess' step before showing the real line.
watch_for: If removing the interactivity doesn't break the story, it's decoration - cut it.

examples.context: An interactive visualizer where the reader manipulates a point-count control and watches the construction's behaviour change as point counts grow - the interactivity carries the explanation rather than decorating it, which is the litmus test for this technique.

'The Reconciliation Engine' is an interactive playground where the reader drives the work - tuning similarity scores and watching messy bank transactions snap onto accounting records. Remove the interactivity and there is no story; the hands-on exploration IS the explanation, which is this technique's litmus test.

name: Statistical Nut Graf
tagline: open with one number that carries the whole story
move: Write a 5-sentence nut graf answering: what changed / by how much / compared to what / who is affected / why now.
No generic adjectives; every sentence must contain a number, entity, place or concrete comparison.
why: Stops 'interesting analysis' from collapsing into vague prose.
try_it: Turn one Statnostics-style insight into a short opener.
watch_for: Don't bury the caveat after making a strong claim.

examples.context: This FLAPS piece is the nut-graf discipline in practice: its intro packs a number into nearly every sentence - Lakshmir Bhandar reaching 2.21 crore women, Rs 26,700 crore annual allocation, a Rs 1,500/month youth allowance, and NSDP growth of 9.86% set against Tamil Nadu's 15.76% and Maharashtra's 11.85% - each line carrying a figure, an entity and a comparison.

name: The Narrative Spine
tagline: chart dump into story
move: Build a 7-beat arc: hook (a person/tension, never a chart) -> why readers care -> main evidence -> counterintuitive twist -> human implication -> caveat/method -> action or question.
Place charts at the moment of revelation, each with a headline that states the finding.
why: Sequence is what converts a chart dump into a story people remember.
try_it: Convert an analysis notebook into seven cards.
watch_for: The 'tale trap': order implies causation. Name the mechanism; let evidence, not sequence, do the work.

examples.context: 'The Paragraph That Appears 418 Times - Wikipedia's Hidden Skeleton' opens on a mystery (a hidden skeleton of human knowledge) and builds to the reveal - 418 asteroid pages sharing a 213-word preamble - rather than front-loading the statistic. A published example of the hook-to-revelation arc.

His data-story skill codifies the spine: open on a person/tension/mystery (never a chart), build through setup -> complication -> revelation -> implications (<=4 beats), and place revelatory (not decorative) charts at the moment of revelation, each with a finding-stating headline.

name: The Angle Generator
tagline: one finding, many framings
move: Ask for a dozen angles for one finding.
For each: who it would interest, why, and the chart idea.
Pick the framing the data can actually carry.
why: The same number is a business, human, or policy story depending on framing.
try_it: Write three headlines for three different audiences from one stat.
watch_for: Don't force an angle the data can't support.

examples.context: The 'Prompt for ideation' applies this to the Goodreads 100k dataset: 'give me a dozen suggestions on A, what stories to write, B, who they would be interesting to and why' - generating many audience-specific framings from one set of columns.

name: Persona Style Transfer
tagline: same evidence, different audience
move: Rewrite a VERIFIED finding in several styles (e.g. wire report, visual essay, policy brief, business memo, social thread).
Preserve facts, numbers and caveats; change only structure, tone and examples.
Freeze the evidence table first.
why: Style is separable from evidence once the facts are locked.
try_it: Write one PLFS insight for a minister, a college student and a newsroom editor.
watch_for: Voice imitation can distort claims - freeze the evidence before transferring style.

examples.context: The FLAPS 'print-to-online' folder (with its own SKILL.md, an originals/ input and an output/ result) is style transfer in practice: the same verified story is re-expressed from a print original into an online format while the underlying facts are held constant.

name: Publish It
tagline: a link beats a screenshot
move: Have the model produce a self-contained hostable page.
Optionally add a gallery and a 'how this was made' process page.
Walk through deploying to GitHub Pages or a Worker.
Strip secrets and large raw files before pushing.
why: A shareable URL turns an exercise into an artifact - ideal for a workshop's 'share your results.'
try_it: Get one story live on a public URL by the end of the session.
watch_for: Strip secrets and large raw data before publishing anything public.

examples.context: The published Statnostics gallery - data-story SVG cards with View Full Size and Verify buttons - deployed straight from the repo to GitHub Pages. The end state this technique produces.

The instruction 'Create a narrative data story explaining how these Statoistics were created... Generate this as statnostics/process.html and link it from the gallery' shows publishing both the stories and a how-it-was-made page - the artifact plus its provenance.

name: Format Transformer
tagline: one verified story, many reader formats
move: Convert one verified story into multiple constrained formats: a 60-second audio script, social cards, an FAQ, a newsletter intro, quote cards, SEO headlines.
Use only facts in the evidence table; add no new claims.
why: Newsrooms use AI for constrained repurposing while the source reporting stays the anchor.
try_it: Convert one workshop story into a 90-second podcast dialogue.
watch_for: Repurposing often smuggles in unsupported context - hold the facts constant.

examples.context: The FLAPS print-to-online pipeline transforms one verified story from a print original into an online output format with the facts held constant - the constrained-repurposing move applied to a real publishing workflow.

name: Human-in-the-Loop Publishing Gate
tagline: decide what AI may do before publishing
move: Before publishing, fill a release card: AI's role (research/cleaning/analysis/code/drafting/editing/viz/repackaging).
List the human checks completed (source, calculation, quotes, named people, legal/ethics, chart accuracy, caveats).
Decide whether disclosure is needed; if so, write the disclosure note.
why: Current newsroom guidance centres human accountability, vetting and disclosure for significant AI use.
try_it: Classify three workshop outputs as needing no / light / clear disclosure.
watch_for: 'AI only helped a little' is not a policy - define the threshold.

examples.context: This file is a working human-review gate: each Hack-of-the-Day card carries a status (Accepted or Rejected, with a reason such as 'published earlier') and a review date/time, recording the human decision that must happen before anything is published - the publishing gate as a concrete artifact.

Each published card's Verify button (linking to its SOP) is the visible half of the same gate: the human-checkable evidence trail that a responsible AI-assisted newsroom attaches before release. (Industry norm: the Guardian uses GenAI only for alt text, headline suggestions and transcription while stories stay human-written - assistive use behind a human gate.)

name: Private-Data Safety Split (Lethal Trifecta)
tagline: avoid the dangerous workflow triangle
move: Label the task on three axes: (1) does it touch private/sensitive data? (2) does it read untrusted content? (3) can it communicate externally or publish?
If all three are true, redesign to remove one leg: anonymise the data, restrict the inputs, or sandbox the outputs.
why: When all three combine, a single poisoned input can make an agent exfiltrate your data via prompt injection.
try_it: Risk-classify five workshop ideas on the three axes.
watch_for: Prompt injection hides in webpages, PDFs, emails and even CSV cells and comments.

examples.context: Simon Willison's 'lethal trifecta' (16 June 2025) defines the exact danger: access to private data + exposure to untrusted content + the ability to communicate externally together let an attacker trick an agent into stealing data. His prescribed mitigation - ensure an agent never has all three at once - is what this technique operationalizes as a pre-task checklist.

'Crack the Prompt' shows why the 'untrusted input' leg is so dangerous: at a PyConf security challenge, terse seven-word prompts pried the hidden system instructions out of three guarded AI personas. It is prompt injection working in practice - the precise threat this checklist is built to keep away from your private data.

name: Three Models, One Question
tagline: ask three AIs the same thing, then have one judge and merge them
move: Optionally sharpen the prompt first by asking one model how an expert editor would brief it.
Run the same research/analysis prompt on ChatGPT, Claude and Gemini.
Paste all answers into a fourth chat: rank them by quality with reasons, rewrite the best combined version, and say which was strongest and why.
why: Models fail differently; an explicit ranking surfaces the best parts and exposes the overconfident one.
try_it: Run one ideation prompt across all three, then have Claude rank and merge.
watch_for: The most fluent answer is often the least reliable - rank on rigor and honesty.

examples.context: The West Bengal 'Compare outputs' section is this technique in full: the same SIR research question is run on ChatGPT, Claude Research, ChatGPT Deep Research and Gemini Deep Research, then Claude Opus 4.6 Extended ranks all four with detailed, dimension-by-dimension reasons and explains why Claude Research was strongest and Gemini weakest - triangulate, rank, merge.

name: Spec Before Code / Meta-Prompt
tagline: have the model write the brief
move: Ask the model to rewrite a rough request as a developer-ready spec: goal, audience, inputs, outputs, visual style, interactions, acceptance tests, edge cases, a 'do not do' list.
Or ask 'How would an expert editor brief this assignment?' and use the rewrite.
Then paste the spec into the executing model.
why: LLMs execute better - and write better prompts - when ambiguity is resolved upstream.
try_it: Take a prompt that underperformed and have the model upgrade it before re-running.
watch_for: A polished spec can still encode the wrong goal - review the problem before building.

examples.context: The 'Meta-prompt' section shows him asking Claude Opus 'Improve this prompt. How would an expert editor brief their investigative journalist?' and the upgraded ROLE/ASSIGNMENT/STORY-REQUIREMENTS brief it returns, which is then used to write the story - the model writing its own better prompt.

The 'Metaprompt to write code' asks the model to write the prompt (complete with GitHub-Pages and <=1-2MB data constraints) that he will then pass to an LLM to generate the visualization code - spec-before-code in a viz workflow.

name: Run in Parallel
tagline: more samples, less waiting
move: Fire several prompts at once across tabs or models.
E.g. one model hunts a dataset while another synthesizes a teaching one; one attempts a known chart while another invents a new one.
Converge from the best of the samples.
why: Parallelism cuts idle time and yields more samples to converge from.
try_it: Race the same task across three tabs.
watch_for: Track which tab is which or you'll merge the wrong outputs.

examples.context: The opening principle is 'Learn fast by pushing LLM limits in parallel': don't wait on a single path - try two or more prompts at once (ask one model to create a SOM, another to invent a new viz; use one for dataset search while another synthesizes a teaching dataset), because 'parallelism = more samples -> better ideas and faster convergence.'

name: The Throwaway Redo
tagline: regenerate, don't repair
move: When an output gets tangled, save any good bits.
Restart from a cleaner, better prompt rather than patching turn by turn.
why: Regenerating from a clean prompt is usually faster and cleaner than debugging a mess.
try_it: Next time you're three 'no, fix this' turns deep, restart instead.
watch_for: Save the good bits first so you don't lose a genuine insight in the reset.

examples.context: Stated directly as a 'Tactics I learnt' lesson between worked examples: 'Throw it away and redo. It's easier than fixing.' - the principle he teaches alongside delegating to code.

name: Build the Reusable Skill
tagline: save the instructions you keep repeating into a reusable cheat-sheet
move: From your transcripts, reviews and past prompts, have the model extract every instruction you repeated 3+ times.
Turn them into a short SKILL.md: principles, examples, anti-patterns, a checklist and a 5-minute exercise.
Keep it short enough to paste into a future chat.
why: Your work speeds up when the judgment in your head becomes reusable notes you can paste into the next chat.
try_it: Build a 'Statnostics one-card' or 'FLAPS headline' skill.
watch_for: Stale skills become dogma - review after every few workshops.

examples.context: This directory holds ~23 SKILL.md files (anand-writing-style, data-analysis, data-story, ideation-protocol, design, demos, cloudflare, devtools and more), each codifying a repeatedly-used judgment into reusable, pasteable context - the technique already running as his personal toolkit.
A domain-specific skill that captures the house style and checklist for FLAPS data-led pieces, so the editorial judgment behind them can be reapplied consistently to new stories.

name: Keep an Impossibility List
tagline: keep a list of what AI can't do yet, and retest it
move: Maintain a running list of things models could not do (a brand-new chart, a tricky analysis).
Retest the list monthly.
why: The capability boundary moves fast; last month's 'impossible' is often this month's one-shot.
try_it: Start the list in the workshop with one thing that failed live.
watch_for: Re-test before declaring something still impossible - your memory of the limit goes stale.

examples.context: Listed as a standing tactic: 'Keep an impossibility list. Review monthly.' - a habit for tracking what LLMs can't yet do.

The talk opens by probing the frontier with 'stretch questions' - asking whether the model can make a SOM or 'a chart newly invented today' (low prior probability of a canned answer) - which is impossibility-testing done live with the class.
